coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of cationic carbon quantum dot-based dual emission fluorescence sensor for detecting perrhenate anions in aqueous solutions
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Mun Ryul
Lee, Byunghwan
coredata.dc:description: This study developed a carbon quantum dot to selectively detect perrhenate anions, which was used as a surrogate for the radioactive pertechnetate anion, in aqueous solutions. A blue emission carbon dot (B-CD) was prepared using a simple microwave technique, whereas a dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) was prepared using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene. When the concentration of the perrhenate anion was increased, both B-CD and D-CD exhibited a decrease in fluorescence brightness owing to the inner filter effect; however, no changes were observed in terms of color. Therefore, cationic D-CDs (C-CDs) were synthesized by introducing quaternary ammonium functional groups on the surface of D-CD to enhance the binding characteristics with the perrhenate anion. At an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, both B-CD and D-CD failed to exhibit a decrease in fluorescence brightness; however, the change in color and reduced brightness of fluorescence were observed in C-CD because of the perrhenate anion. The change in fluorescence color can be attributed to the difference in the intensity reduction in dual-emission wavelength regions. Furthermore, C-CD exhibited linear correlations for perrhenate anion concentrations of up to 1 mM, and the limits of detection were 87 and 208 μM at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 350 nm, respectively. Additionally, the results indicate that C-CD can be used as a sensor to detect perrhenate anions despite the presence of excess chloride anions. This fluorescence change can be attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer mechanism caused by the electrostatic interactions between the cationic surface functional groups of C-CD and perrhenate anions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cationic carbon dot
Dual-emission
Fluorescence
Perrhenate anion
Color change

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ), grant funded by the Korea government ( MSIT ) (grant number 2020R1F1A1048770 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of a cationic dual-emission carbon dot (C-CD).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of (a) blue emission carbon dot (B-CD), (b) dual-emission CD (D-CD), and (c and d) cationic D-CD (C-CD), and (e) fast Fourier transform pattern of the C-CD.
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of a (a) dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) and (b) cationic D-CD (C-CD).
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of a (a) dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) and (b) cationic D-CD (C-CD).
High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of (a) C 1s and (b) N 1s of dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) and (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s of cationic D-CD (C-CD).
Zeta potential distributions of the aqueous dispersed (a) dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) and (b) cationic D-CD (C-CD). (c) Zeta potentials of D-CD and C-CD.

(a) Fluorescence intensities of the blue emission carbon dot (B-CD) at an excitation wavelength of 254 nm according to the anion species, including deionized water, ReO4 −, I−, Cl−, HCO3 −, CH3COO−, CN−, and F−, and (b) fluorescence intensity change ratios of B-CD according to the perrhenate anion concentrations. Inset shows the fluorescence intensity changes of B-CD.
Fluorescence intensities of a dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) according to the perrhenate anion concentrations of (A) 0 mM and (B) 50 mM at excitation wavelengths of (a) 254, (b) 275, and (c) 350 nm.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectra of anion aqueous solutions of (A) ReO4 −, (B) I−, (C) Cl−, (D) HCO3 −, (E) CH3COO−, (F) CN−, and (G) F− and excitation spectra of cationic dual-emission carbon dot (C-CD) for emission wavelengths of (H) 450 and (I) 540 nm.

(a) Fluorescence images of cationic dual-emission carbon dots (C-CDs) according to the anion species, including deionized water, ReO4 −, I−, Cl−, HCO3 −, CH3COO−, CN−, and F−. Fluorescence intensities of C-CDs at excitation wavelengths of (b) 254, (c) 275, and (d) 350 nm for the anion species. (e) Fluorescence intensity change ratios at 540 nm for the excitation wavelengths of 254 (■), 275 (■), and 350 nm (■).

Fluorescence intensity change ratios of a cationic dual-emission carbon dot (C-CD) according to the perrhenate anion concentrations at excitation wavelengths of (a) 254, (b) 275, and (c) 350 nm. Inset shows the fluorescence intensity changes of C-CD.

Fluorescence intensity change ratios of a cationic dual-emission carbon dot (C-CD) according to the perrhenate anion concentrations in the presence of 0.1 M chloride anions. Inset shows the fluorescence intensity changes of C-CD.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectra of a (a) cationic dual-emission carbon dot (C-CD) aqueous solution (25 ppm), (b) perrhenate anion aqueous solution (50 mM), and (c) C-CD aqueous solution with perrhenate anions.
Schematic of the fluorescence quenching mechanism based on perrhenate anions.
Trend line parameters of fluorescence intensity change ratios of cationic dual-emission carbon dots (C-CDs) based on the perrhenate anion concentrations at various excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of cationic carbon quantum dot-based dual emission fluorescence sensor for detecting perrhenate anions in aqueous solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Keimyung University, 1095 Dalgubeoldaero, Dalseo-gu, Daegu, 42601, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Keimyung University, 1095 Dalgubeoldaero, Dalseo-gu, Daegu, 42601, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study developed a carbon quantum dot to selectively detect perrhenate anions, which was used as a surrogate for the radioactive pertechnetate anion, in aqueous solutions. A blue emission carbon dot (B-CD) was prepared using a simple microwave technique, whereas a dual-emission carbon dot (D-CD) was prepared using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene. When the concentration of the perrhenate anion was increased, both B-CD and D-CD exhibited a decrease in fluorescence brightness owing to the inner filter effect; however, no changes were observed in terms of color. Therefore, cationic D-CDs (C-CDs) were synthesized by introducing quaternary ammonium functional groups on the surface of D-CD to enhance the binding characteristics with the perrhenate anion. At an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, both B-CD and D-CD failed to exhibit a decrease in fluorescence brightness; however, the change in color and reduced brightness of fluorescence were observed in C-CD because of the perrhenate anion. The change in fluorescence color can be attributed to the difference in the intensity reduction in dual-emission wavelength regions. Furthermore, C-CD exhibited linear correlations for perrhenate anion concentrations of up to 1 mM, and the limits of detection were 87 and 208 μM at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 350 nm, respectively. Additionally, the results indicate that C-CD can be used as a sensor to detect perrhenate anions despite the presence of excess chloride anions. This fluorescence change can be attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer mechanism caused by the electrostatic interactions between the cationic surface functional groups of C-CD and perrhenate anions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Several heavy metal oxide ions generated by industrial processes are considered environmental pollutants and toxins that damage the ecosystem [1,2]. Among these ions, the pertechnetate anion (TcO4 −) is one of the most dangerous environmental pollutants owing to its radioactivity and long half-life [1,3]. Technetium occurs naturally as a stable oxidized form of the pertechnetate anion and is a major by-product in the nuclear industry, predominantly generated during the radioactive decay of uranium [4]. Pertechnetate anions exhibit high stability, physiological toxicity, and high solubility in water (11.3 mol/L at 20 °C) [5]. They exist at concentrations of several millimoles in a treatment plant that handles radioactive wastes [6]. High concentrations of pertechnetate anions leak into groundwater, directly affecting aquatic organisms and ecosystems linked to the food chain, with consequences that eventually reach humans [1]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a technique for the detection and appropriate treatment of pertechnetate anions that are discharged into water.

Employing the pertechnetate anion directly in experiments is difficult because it is categorized as a radioactive material. Typically, studies on the detection of pertechnetate anions have been conducted using non-radioactive surrogates in a form similar to that of pertechnetate anions [7]. The pertechnetate and perrhenate anions (ReO4 −) exhibit similar physical properties. Owing to identical geometry, similar size (dTc-O = 1.71 Å and dRe-O = 1.72 Å to 1.73 Å), and similar hydration energy (ΔHhyd, TcO4 − = −251 kJ/mol and ΔHhyd, ReO4 − = −330 kJ/mol), pertechnetate and perrhenate anion detection methods are considered nearly identical [8,9]. As the perrhenate anion has been previously used as a non-radioactive surrogate for the pertechnetate anion in several studies [1,4,7,10,11], the same was followed in this study.

Analytical methods used for anion detection include ion chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography combined with atomic absorption spectroscopy, and chemiluminescence sensors [12]. Typically, anion detection methods using chromatography exhibit high accuracy and detection sensitivity. However, these methods are time-consuming because the column and material to be detected must sufficiently interact for the separation to occur. ICP-MS ionizes a sample and detects the ion with high precision and sensitivity using a mass spectrometer; however, operating the expensive equipment requires professional personnel, which incurs high costs. Conversely, the chemiluminescent sensor ensures fast detection through fluorescence or color change, exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity, and is inexpensive. Therefore, several studies explored the detection of anions using chemiluminescent sensors. Recent studies reported the detection of anions using hydrogen bonding, coordination bonding, and electrostatic interactions in non-aqueous media [13,14] or aqueous solutions [15,16] with chemiluminescent compounds, such as p-xylyl cryptand and copper-based tripodal complexes.

Quantum dots (QDs), composed of inorganic or carbon-based compounds, are materials with zero-dimensional structures and the size of a few nanometers. The band gap energy of QDs varies with the particle size, which in turn changes the fluorescence wavelength. The decrease and increase in the size of QD result in the emission of visible light of short and long wavelengths, such as blue and red, respectively. As QDs can emit light in the visible region by simply adjusting their size and remaining unaffected by their components, they are used for various applications, such as semiconductor devices, photocatalysts, and sensors [17–19]. Inorganic QDs primarily use toxic heavy metals; therefore, carbon QDs (CQDs) with relatively low toxicity are increasingly researched. CQDs are carbon-based nanomaterials comprising hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen; they exhibit semiconducting properties similar to those of inorganic QDs. The bandgap properties of CQDs rely on the internal delocalized electron pairs and various functional groups on the surface. Moreover, controlling the bandgap of CQDs is more difficult than controlling that of inorganic QDs. Most CQDs do not emit light in the long-wavelength region, and the exact fluorescence emission mechanism remains unclear. However, it has certain advantages, such as low production costs and the ability to combine various functional groups [20–23].

Studies that detect perrhenate anions using chemiluminescence sensors predominantly used auramine O, thioflavin-T, 1-pyrenemethylamine, and Zr4+ metal-organic framework as phosphors. The linear ranges for perrhenate anion detection in different studies were 30–100, 10–50, 0.5–40, and 0.2–20 mM, with detection limits of 270, 260, 1.3, and 14 μM, respectively [1,4,7,11]. However, compared to CQDs, these phosphor materials are rather expensive. Additionally, many studies have been performed on utilizing different fluorescent dyes to implement color changes, but research on employing CQDs to implement such color changes is insufficient. Only a few studies focused on the detection of perrhenate anions using CQDs as chemiluminescence-based sensors. Although certain studies have reported the detection of iodide anions and heavy metal ions, the iodide anion was detected using methods in which the fluorescence returned with the removal of heavy metal from the iodide anion, in contrast to the decrease in fluorescence that occurred when the heavy metal was bonded [24,25]. Therefore, an inexpensive material with significant fluorescence changes is required for the efficient detection of perrhenate anions. This study reports the fabrication of a fluorescent sensor using CQDs to improve the existing perrhenate anion detection methods. CQDs with double-emission wavelengths were used to induce the changes of the fluorescence intensity and its color. Furthermore, the surface functional groups of the CQDs were adjusted to generate a positive charge on the surface and induce direct interaction between the CQDs and anions.

Citric acid (HOC(CO2H)(CH2CO2H)2, >99.5%; Sigma-Aldrich, USA), urea (NH2CONH2, 99.0–100.5%; Sigma-Aldrich, USA), and deionized water were used for the blue emission carbon dot (B-CD) synthesis. Additionally, pyrene (C16H10, 98%; Sigma-Aldrich, USA), nitric acid (HNO3, 60.0%; Samchun Pure Chemical Co., Ltd., Korea), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%; OCI Company Ltd., Korea) were used for the synthesis of dual-emission carbon dots (D-CDs). Glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride (C6H14ClNO, >99.5%; Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was used for the surface modification of the D-CD. Furthermore, nitrocellulose membrane filter (0.22 μm; Sigma-Aldrich, USA), silica 60 (0.063–0.200 mm, 70–230 mesh ASTM, Merck KGaA, Germany), and ethanol (C2H5OH, 99%; Samchun Pure Chemical Co., Ltd., Korea) were used to purify the prepared CQDs.

The target materials for fluorescence experiments included sodium perrhenate (NaReO4, 99.99%), sodium iodide (NaI, >99%), sodium chloride (NaCl, >99.0%), and sodium acetate (CH3COONa, >99%) supplied by Sigma-Aldrich, USA; and sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, 99%), sodium cyanide (NaCN, 97%), and sodium fluoride (NaF, 97%) supplied by Samchun Pure Chemical Co., Ltd., Korea.

B-CDs with a single emission peak at 450 nm were prepared using citric acid and urea as precursors [26], wherein the citric acid (0.19 g) and urea (0.06 g) were dissolved in deionized water (5 mL). This solution was irradiated with microwaves at 750 W for 4 min. After re-dispersing the prepared material in 5 mL of deionized water, insoluble hydrocarbons were removed using a syringe filter (0.2 μm; Sartorius AG, Germany). The aqueous solution was dried for 24 h using a freeze dryer (TFD5503, IlShinBioBase, Korea) to obtain the B-CD powder.

D-CDs exhibiting dual-emission peaks at 450 and 540 nm were prepared using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as a precursor [27]; herein, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was synthesized by the nitridation of pyrene with an aqueous nitric acid solution as reported in a previous study [28]. The prepared 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.5 g) was placed in a 0.2-M-aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (100 mL) and dispersed for 2 h using a sonicator (40 kHz; U1TECH, Korea). This solution was placed in a Teflon bath for autoclaving and carbonized at 200 °C for 10 h. The solution was cooled to 20 °C and filtered under reduced pressure using a 0.22-μm-membrane filter to remove insoluble hydrocarbons. The solution was concentrated using a rotary evaporator, and the impurities were removed using silica gel column chromatography. Silica 60 and ethanol were used as the stationary and mobile phases, respectively. Subsequently, the solution was concentrated again using a rotary evaporator before dispersing in deionized water (20 mL). Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the D-CD powder.

A cationic D-CD (C-CD) was prepared by introducing quaternary ammonium functional groups onto the surface of the prepared D-CD [29]. The D-CD (0.25 g) was dispersed in 250 mL of deionized water, and 0.5 mL of glycidyltrimethylammonium chloride was added. The solution was initially stirred at 20 °C for 2 h and then stirred at 80 °C for 6 h. The solution was cooled to 20 °C and filtered under reduced pressure using a 0.22-μm-membrane filter to remove impurities. Furthermore, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min to remove aggregated carbon compounds, and the supernatant was collected and concentrated using a rotary evaporator. The concentrate was dispersed in ethanol to replace the solvent and filtered under reduced pressure to remove the impurities. Finally, the solution was concentrated at 60 °C using a rotary evaporator and dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C to obtain the C-CD powder (Fig. 1 ).

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (JNM-ECZ500R, JEOL Ltd., Japan) and high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS; JMS-700, JEOL Ltd., Japan) were used to confirm the synthesis of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, a precursor for D-CD preparation. A field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM; Titan G2 ChemiSTEM Cs Probe, FEI Company, USA) was used to validate the crystal structure and particle size of the prepared materials. The surface functional groups and chemical bonding states of the prepared materials were examined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS50, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher, NEXSA, USA). A zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano ZS, UK) was used to measure the surface charges of the prepared materials. Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis; UV-1650, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to confirm the absorption wavelengths of the CQDs and anions used in the study.

Fluorescence experiments were performed using a fluorescence lifetime imaging microscope (FLIM; MicroTime 200, PicoQuant, Germany) to confirm that the fluorescence of the CQDs changed depending on the type of anion in the aqueous solution and the excitation wavelength. Blank measurements were performed 10 times using a 25-ppm-aqueous CQD solution before the experiments.

B-CD and an aqueous solution containing each anion, including ReO4 −, I−, Cl−, HCO3 −, CH3COO−, CN−, and F− were mixed and excited at a wavelength of 254 nm to measure the emission wavelengths. The concentrations of B-CD and anion in the mixed solutions were 25 ppm and 50 mM, respectively.

Aqueous solutions containing D-CD and perrhenate anions were excited at 254, 275, and 350 nm to determine the change in fluorescence emission of D-CD with respect to the perrhenate anion according to the excitation wavelength. The concentrations of D-CD and perrhenate anions in the mixed solutions were 25 ppm and 50 mM, respectively.

Similarly, fluorescence experiments were performed using C-CD to confirm the selectivity for each anion and the change in fluorescence based on the excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the change in fluorescence as a function of perrhenate anion concentrations (0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mM) was evaluated using C-CD. The changes in C-CD fluorescence were experimentally evaluated considering the changes in the concentration of the perrhenate anion in an environment with a background ion. The emission wavelength was measured by exciting an aqueous solution containing C-CD at a wavelength of 350 nm while varying the concentrations of the perrhenate anion using a 0.1-M-chloride anion as a background ion.

The results of NMR and HRMS analyses indicate that 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was successfully synthesized, verifying that the precursors for D-CD and C-CD syntheses were prepared accurately, as described in a previous study [28].

TEM images of all the prepared CQDs were shown in Fig. 2 . The results of FE-TEM analyses confirm the particle size and crystallinity of the prepared C-CD. C-CD was spherical with a diameter of 2–5 nm. A typical lattice pattern with a spacing of 0.207 nm was observed and the diffraction pattern of CQDs exhibited a graphene-like structure [27].

To confirm whether the quaternary ammonium group was accurately introduced to the surface of C-CD, the chemical bonding states of D-CD and C-CD were analyzed using FT-IR. Fig. 3 depicts the results measured by the attenuated total reflection method using a diamond module. The FT-IR spectrum of D-CD exhibited OH peaks at 3580 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1, a CH3 peak at 1430 cm−1, a CH peak at 880 cm−1, and out-of-plane CH peaks at 780 cm−1 and 630 cm−1 [27]. However, the OH peaks at 3580 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1, which occurred in D-CD, diminished in C-CD, whereas the CH3 and CH peaks at 1430 and 880 cm−1, respectively, became stronger. These FT-IR measurements indicate that the CH3 and CH functional groups increased on the C-CD surface as the D-CD surface, which was rich in OH functional groups, was functionalized using quaternary ammonium groups.

Furthermore, XPS was used to examine the surface functional groups of D-CD and C-CD to ensure the accurate introduction of quaternary ammonium groups onto the surface of C-CD. Fig. 4 depicts the XPS survey spectra of D-CD and C-CD, wherein C, N, and O elements were analyzed. In the XPS survey spectra, peaks for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Na 1s were identified at 284.98, 399.54, 530.27, and 1071.80 eV, respectively. The Na peak was generated by the aqueous sodium hydroxide solution used in the preparation of D-CD. As the quaternary ammonium group was introduced, the atomic percentage of nitrogen increased from 1.42% in D-CD to 3.18% in C-CD, whereas that of carbon increased from 53.3% in D-CD to 67.0% in C-CD. Additionally, the atomic percentage of oxygen decreased from 45.2% in D-CD to 29.8% in C-CD. These results verify that the quaternary ammonium functional groups were successfully bound to the C-CD surface.

The chemical bonding of the atoms comprising the surface functional groups of D-CD and C-CD was validated using high-resolution XPS (Fig. 5 ). The C 1s high-resolution spectrum of D-CD confirmed the CC and CO binding energies at 284.43 and 288.50 eV, respectively. Additionally, the C–NC and N–3C binding energies were confirmed at 398.34 and 400.75 eV respectively in the N 1s high-resolution spectrum of D-CD. The C 1s high-resolution spectrum of C-CD confirmed the CC and CO binding energies at 284.29 and 288.46 eV, respectively. In the N 1s high-resolution spectrum, C–NC and N+-R4 binding energies were confirmed at 397.88 and 402.95 eV, respectively [30]. The comparison of D-CD and C-CD indicated that the peak of the CO binding energy in the C 1s spectrum of C-CD was lower than that observed in D-CD. Furthermore, the peak of the binding energy of N+-R4 at 402.95 eV in the N 1s spectrum was confirmed to be new in C-CD. These data indicate that the D-CD surface, which was rich in oxygen functional groups, was successfully functionalized using quaternary ammonium groups, resulting in C-CD.

The surface charge of C-CD is expected to change from negative to positive as quaternary ammonium functional groups are introduced. The zeta potential was measured to confirm the difference in the surface charge between D-CD and C-CD. Fig. 6 depicts the results of multiple measurements obtained using Zetasizer. Both D-CD and C-CD solutions were prepared at 50 ppm and used for the analysis. Although C-CD exhibited an unstable colloidal form with a wide symmetric distribution (Fig. 6(b)), extremely small-sized CQDs were uniformly dispersed in an aqueous solution with no precipitate formation even after 14 days, demonstrating the excellent dispersibility of C-CD. The surface charges of D-CD and C-CD were −25.2 and 0.632 mV, respectively (Fig. 6(c)). The comparison of the surface charge measurements of D-CD and C-CD indicated that the surface charge of C-CD changed to positive owing to the introduction of quaternary ammonium groups to the negatively charged D-CD surface. These findings confirm the presence of quaternary ammonium functional groups on the surface of C-CD. Furthermore, the interactions with bulky anions were possible because the surface was positively charged.

Furthermore, experiments were conducted to determine the fluorescence change in B-CD with the increase in the perrhenate anion concentration from 0.05 to 50 mM, with the B-CD concentration set to 25 ppm in the aqueous solution. Fig. 7(b) depicts the measured fluorescence spectra, wherein the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase in the concentration of the perrhenate anions. The fluorescence intensity change ratio can be expressed as 1−(F/F0) to obtain a linear range of change in fluorescence intensity; here, F0 denotes the fluorescence intensity at an emission wavelength of 450 nm when the concentration of the perrhenate anion is 0 mM and F indicates the fluorescence intensity at each concentration of the perrhenate anion. The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained using 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the background experiment and S denotes the slope of the change in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity change exhibited a linear relationship with perrhenate anion concentration within 1 mM, wherein the average R2 value was the highest across several experiments. The R2 value and slope of the fluorescence intensity change as a function of perrhenate anion concentration were 0.9712 and 0.6874 in the linear range, respectively, indicating strong linearity at LOD of 67 μM. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased across the entire emission spectrum in the B-CD (Fig. 7(b)), indicating that the fluorescence brightness was reduced. Therefore, experiments were performed to implement both the brightness and color change of the fluorescence.

D-CD was synthesized with dual emission characteristics using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, with the expectation that D-CD would change its fluorescence color in response to the changes in the perrhenate anion concentration [27,28]. The D-CD exhibited dual emission wavelengths with maximum emissions at 450 and 540 nm. Fluorescence experiments were performed to confirm the change in the fluorescence intensity when the perrhenate anion was present and investigate the change in fluorescence properties as a function of the excitation wavelength. The concentration of D-CD in the mixed aqueous solution was set to 25 ppm, whereas the concentrations of perrhenate anions were set to 0 and 50 mM. The fluorescence of these solutions was measured while varying the excitation wavelength; Fig. 8 depicts the corresponding results. Fig. 8(a) and (b) indicate that the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly when the aqueous solution was excited at wavelengths of 254 and 275 nm, respectively. However, no change was observed in the fluorescence intensity when the aqueous solution was excited at a wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 8(c)). Despite the preparation of CQD with a double emission wavelength, the fluorescence intensity decreased in D-CD across the entire emission spectrum, similar to that observed in B-CD, resulting in only a change in fluorescence brightness. However, controlling the excitation wavelength exhibited the possibility of selectively detecting perrhenate anions.

Although the B-CD and D-CD exhibited fluorescence reduction for perrhenate anions at a specific excitation wavelength, no change was observed in fluorescence color. To enhance the interaction with the target substance (the perrhenate anion) and change the fluorescence color based on the reaction, a C-CD was prepared using quaternary ammonium functional groups [29]. The fluorescence properties of C-CD were investigated considering the anion type and excitation wavelength. The concentrations of C-CD and anion were set to 25 ppm and 50 mM in the experiment, respectively. C-CD exhibited dual emission wavelength characteristics, with emission peaks at 450 and 540 nm. Fig. 10 depicts the effects of changing the type of anion and excitation wavelength on fluorescence. When excited at a wavelength of 254 nm, the fluorescence intensity in the sample containing perrhenate and iodide anions decreased significantly. These experimental results were comparable to the D-CD results. However, the fluorescence color of the sample containing the perrhenate anion changed from cyan to blue when the C-CD was activated at a wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 10(a)). The color shift was not sufficiently evident because complete quenching of the emission of a specific wavelength region was not achieved (Fig. 10(d)). However, it was confirmed that the perrhenate anion caused the fluorescence color change of C-CD unlike B-CD and D-CD.

The fluorescence emission characteristics of C-CD with respect to the anions at various excitation wavelengths were examined using FLIM to quantitatively investigate this color change. When C-CD was excited at wavelengths of 254 and 275 nm, results similar to those of the D-CD experiment were observed. In samples containing perrhenate and iodide anions, the whole fluorescence intensity decreased drastically at an excitation wavelength of 254 nm (Fig. 10(b)). At 275 nm, a sharp decrease in the whole fluorescence intensity occurred only in the sample containing the perrhenate anion (Fig. 10(c)). However, at the excitation wavelength of 275 nm, the emission spectrum at 450 nm was very weak, indicating that the dual-emission characteristic became weaker. Those fluorescence decreases can be attributed to perrhenate anions absorbing light with a wavelength of 300 nm or less, and the iodide anion absorbing light with a wavelength of 260 nm or less. Fig. 9 shows the excitation spectra of C-CD together with the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of anions. C-CD showed excitation wavelengths of 240 and 278 nm with respect to emission wavelengths of 450 and 540 nm, respectively, suggesting that perrhenate anion prevent the emission of C-CD in this excitation wavelength region. When aqueous solutions were excited at 350 nm, which is a wavelength that does not exhibit an inner filter effect, no reduction was observed in the fluorescence intensity because of perrhenate anions in the D-CD. However, the emission peak intensity decreased at 540 nm in C-CD owing to the presence of the perrhenate anion causing the color change (Fig. 10(d)).

To investigate the selectivity of C-CD to perrhenate anion, the change in fluorescence intensity of C-CD for each anion was measured. The concentrations of C-CD and each anion in the mixed solutions were 50 ppm and 50 mM, respectively. Fig. 10(e) depicts the fluorescence intensity change ratio of C-CD at an emission wavelength of 540 nm as a function of anion type and excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity change ratio for the perrhenate and iodide anions was nearly 1.0 at an excitation wavelength of 254 nm. At 275 nm, only the perrhenate anion exhibited a decrease in fluorescence intensity; whereas, at 350 nm, the fluorescence intensity change ratio for the perrhenate anion was 0.61, which was 1.9 times that of the iodide anion (0.32) and nearly five times more selective than the other anions (0.10–0.14). This demonstrates that C-CD has a higher fluorescence selectivity for the perrhenate anion than for other anions.

C-CD exhibited a preferential decrease in fluorescence intensity at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 275 nm. Furthermore, a change in fluorescence color was observed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm with respect to perrhenate anions. Experiments were performed to determine the change in C-CD fluorescence intensity as a function of the perrhenate anion concentration in the aqueous solution. The C-CD concentration in the aqueous solution was 25 ppm, whereas the perrhenate anion concentration ranged from 0.05 to 50 mM. Fig. 11 depicts the measured fluorescence spectra. Even at a perrhenate anion concentration of 5 mM, the fluorescence decreased substantially at an excitation wavelength of 254 nm (Fig. 11(a)). The fluorescence intensity decreased as the concentration of perrhenate anions increased at all three excitation wavelengths used in the experiments. However, the emission spectra measured at 350 nm exhibited a different type of fluorescence intensity reduction than those measured at 254 and 275 nm. The fluorescence intensity decreased over the entire emission wavelength region at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 275 nm. However, at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 540 nm was larger than that at 450 nm (Fig. 11(c)). Therefore, the fluorescence color change can be attributed to a difference in the fluorescence intensity reduction in the dual-emission wavelength region.

Fig. 11 depicts the fluorescence intensity change ratios at excitation wavelengths of 254, 275, and 350 nm according to the concentration of perrhenate anions. Table 1 summarizes the relationship between the perrhenate anion concentration and the fluorescence intensity change ratio along with the trend line equation and R2. The linear range of the fluorescence intensity change was set to 1 mM at the maximum R2 value. The R2 values of the trend line equation were 0.9906 and 0.9434 at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 350 nm, respectively, indicating strong linear correlations. The values of LOD of C-CD for the perrhenate anion were 87 and 208 μM at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 350 nm, respectively. The LOD values obtained in this study for perrhenate anions were similar to or lower than those reported in the literature for a sensor with organic dyes as phosphors (auramine O, 270 μM [1]; thioflavin T, 260 μM [4]; and 1-pyrenemethylamine, 14 μM [7]). C-CD exhibited an LOD value larger than that of a sensor using a metal-organic framework as a phosphor (Zr4+ MOF, 0.56 μM [11]). However, the metal-organic framework exhibits certain disadvantages, such as low chemical stability and moisture resistance, whereas the proposed CQD overcomes these disadvantages. Additionally, immediate validation of the presence or absence of perrhenate anions in aqueous solutions was possible in this study owing to the change in the fluorescence color of C-CD at an excitation wavelength of relatively low energy (350 nm). However, it is suggested that further research is required to expand the concentration range of C-CD that exhibits linear relations with respect to perrhenate anion concentrations.

Further experiments were performed to determine whether the fluorescence characteristics of C-CD changed in the presence of background ions with respect to the perrhenate anion. Chlorine, which is the most prevalent ion in seawater, was used as the background ion. The concentrations of C-CD and chloride anions were maintained at 25 ppm and 0.1 M respectively, whereas the concentration of perrhenate anion was varied from 0.1 to 50 mM to measure the emission fluorescence. The aqueous solution was excited at a wavelength of 350 nm immediately after mixing to measure the fluorescence. Fig. 12 depicts the measured fluorescence spectra and the trend of the fluorescence intensity changes. This result was similar to the change in fluorescence intensity when background ions were absent (Fig. 11(c)). The LOD value was 229 μM, which was not substantially different from the result when background ions were absent. Therefore, the results verify that C-CD can be employed as a sensor to detect perrhenate anions even in the presence of excessive chloride anions.

In this study, quaternary ammonium groups were functionalized on the surface of D-CD with dual emission properties to generate a C-CD. Additionally, the perrhenate anion was identified by fluorescence in an aqueous solution. At an excitation wavelength of 254 nm, C-CD exhibited a preferential decrease in the fluorescence intensity. In contrast to B-CD and D-CD, C-CD exhibited a change in fluorescence color for the perrhenate anion at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which can be attributed to a difference in fluorescence intensity reduction in the dual-emission wavelength region. At excitation wavelengths of 254 and 350 nm, the R2 values of the trend line equation were 0.9906 and 0.9434, respectively, indicating strong linear correlations. Furthermore, the values of LOD of C-CD for the perrhenate anion were 87 and 208 μM at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 350 nm, respectively. The LOD values for the perrhenate anion obtained in this study were comparable to or less than those reported in the literature. Furthermore, the experimental results verify that C-CD can be used as a sensor to detect perrhenate anions despite the presence of large amounts of chloride anions. The fluorescent sensor fabricated using CQDs can improve the existing perrhenate anion detection methods. C-CD with a double emission wavelength reduced the fluorescence intensity and resulted in a fluorescence color change. Additionally, the surface functional group of C-CD was adjusted to induce a positive charge on the surface to facilitate direct contact between C-CD and the perrhenate anion. Therefore, the developed C-CD enables quantitative determination of the concentration while visually verifying the radioactive anion. However, to expand the concentration range of C-CD that displays linear relations with respect to perrhenate anion concentrations, further research is required.
Mun Ryul Choi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Byunghwan Lee: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (grant number 2020R1F1A1048770).
